context: Current closing price of DuPont (DD): $46.71 USD as of 2026-04-21.
justification_text:
The point estimates in this forecast are based on the best available current trends and expected dynamics over increasing time horizons. For the 1-day estimate, the forecast centers tightly on the most recent observed value, as there is little time for major changes to materialize. The 1-week and 1-month estimates maintain a similar trendline but incorporate the possibility of small directional deviations as more time passes—this aligns with the natural progression of uncertainty in forecasting the future. Over the course of one month, the point estimate assumes that underlying factors such as demand and supply, or other relevant drivers of this variable, will stabilize near recent levels, with no drastic structural shifts anticipated.

The main driver of the confidence interval widths is the degree of uncertainty and variability inherent in the system over time. In the short term (e.g., 1 day), fewer unknowns are at play, and there is less time for unexpected factors to take effect. This is why the confidence interval around the point estimate is relatively narrow. As the time horizon extends to one week and one month, more variables—whether random shocks, seasonal effects, or outside influences—come into play, causing the confidence intervals to widen. The month-long forecast shows the broadest range since there is more time for potential deviations from the expected path.

There are, however, scenarios that could push the outcomes outside of the stated intervals. For example, an unforeseen event such as a major geopolitical incident, economic policy announcement, or significant natural disaster could drastically alter the forecasted values. Similarly, if there is an extreme market movement or an unexpected external shock that was not factored into the initial modeling, the actual outcome could fall well outside the bounds of confidence intervals, especially at the longer time horizons. Such risks underscore the probabilistic nature of the forecast and the inherent limitations of projecting the future.
